endings.title: The Ghost Between the Walls
endings.text: They found ledgers in the utility closet that predated the mall's construction. Receipts for transactions in stores that never existed, signed by managers who never clocked in. You owned every unit on the directory and still couldn't explain the one that wasn't listed. The fluorescent lights in that corridor never needed replacing.

endings.title: The Mall Legend Redux
endings.text: The custodial staff started leaving notes in your suggestion box — not complaints, but coordinates. Each one led to something the mall had buried: a walled-off arcade, a fountain that ran backward, a directory listing for a store that sold only static. You followed every thread and the mall revealed itself completely. Revenue was incidental. You became the thing the building remembered.

endings.title: Lights Out
endings.text: The register never warmed up. Seven days of unlocking the gate, flipping the sign, and watching the corridor stay empty. The mall custodian collected your keys with the efficiency of someone who keeps a spare set ready. Your name was off the directory before lunch.

endings.title: Foreclosure
endings.text: Two weeks of diminishing returns and expanding silence. The regulars stopped coming first, then the walk-ins thinned, then the corridor outside your unit started feeling longer than it used to. Management slid the eviction notice under the gate at 6 AM on a Tuesday. The envelope was warm from the copier. They had been ready for days.

endings.title: Going, Going, Gone
endings.text: You held on past the point where holding on made financial sense and into the territory where it became a philosophical position. Fifteen days of watching the numbers contract. The last customer bought a clearance item out of what felt like pity. When you finally locked the gate for good, the click echoed further than it should have.

endings.title: The Prestige Champion
endings.text: Fifty thousand in revenue, a reputation that preceded you into every conversation, and a customer base whose satisfaction rate would embarrass a five-star hotel. The mall hadn't seen numbers like yours since the building's peak decade. Other tenants framed your monthly figures and used them as aspirational targets. The corporate office sent a representative who took notes and didn't say much. You had built something that the space itself seemed reluctant to let end.

endings.title: The Local Legend
endings.text: The food court regulars knew your name. The parking lot attendants waved when you pulled in. One store, run well enough and long enough that it stopped being a business and started being a landmark. People gave directions using your storefront as a reference point. That kind of reputation doesn't show up on a balance sheet, but it outlasts everything that does.

endings.title: The Mini Empire
endings.text: Three storefronts. Three sets of keys, three gate codes, three morning routines that blurred into a single controlled orbit through the building. The other tenants started treating you differently — not with deference exactly, but with the cautious respect given to someone who clearly understood something about this place that they did not.

endings.title: The Mall Tycoon
endings.text: Every unit on the directory bore your name. The building manager stopped scheduling tenant meetings because there was only one tenant and you already knew what you thought. Twenty-five thousand in revenue and a corridor that belonged to you in every sense that mattered. The escalators carried people to your stores because there was nowhere else to go.

endings.title: The Fair Dealer
endings.text: Two hundred customers walked out satisfied, and not a single one was haggled down or pressured up. The posted price was the price. People talked about it — in a retail ecosystem built on negotiation and margin games, you ran a fixed-price operation and made it work. The regulars trusted your tags the way they trusted the scale at the deli counter.

endings.title: The Collector
endings.text: Ten rare pieces moved through your inventory and into the hands of people who understood what they were holding. Each transaction felt less like a sale and more like a custody transfer. The collectors knew your store by reputation before they knew it by name. Your shelves told a rotating story of scarcity and provenance that the other tenants couldn't replicate.

endings.title: Broke Even
endings.text: Thirty days and the ledger balanced to a number that was technically positive and spiritually neutral. You covered rent, paid the lights, moved enough product to justify the shelf space, and emerged on the other side with your deposit intact. The mall directory still listed you. That was the whole achievement, and it was enough.

endings.title: The Comfortable Middle
endings.text: Not the top tenant, not the bottom. Somewhere in the middle of the directory where the font size was the same as everyone else's. Revenue cleared the threshold where management stopped checking in and fell short of the threshold where they started paying attention. You occupied the zone of comfortable anonymity, and the rent checks cleared every month without drama.

endings.title: Crisis Operator
endings.text: Ten days below the bankruptcy line and you never crossed it. The register balance dipped into the red zone with the regularity of a tide table, and each time you pulled it back with a sale that arrived exactly when the math said it shouldn't. Thirty days of operating on the edge taught you something about retail that comfortable margins never could. You survived on timing and nerve.
